sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 46, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has a left panel (charcoal bg) with various colored objects and a right panel (black bg) with two white-framed target shapes (red on top, blue on bottom). The separator is a column of white 2x2 dots. Valid actions include arrow keys and MOUSE.
- Goal model: Likely need to match/move colored objects from left to right panels, or select objects to fill the target frames.
- Action model: Unknown - need to probe with arrow keys and MOUSE to understand controls.
- Plan: First probe with RIGHT to check if there's a moving cursor/player, then try MOUSE on objects.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 45 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The RIGHT action only changed row 63, col 29 from 'W' to 'G'. This is at the bottom of the board, likely a HUD element (timer/progress bar). So the arrow keys don't move any objects on the board - they just advance the HUD timer. This means the arrow keys are wasted moves!
So the only meaningful actions are MOUSE clicks on the targets. Let me reconsider the puzzle.
Current state:
- Red block at (30, 12)-(33, 17) - horizontal position (position 2)
- Blue square at (20, 18)-(23, 21) - top of middle column (position 2)
- Checkerboard at (34, 8)-(37, 11) - bottom-left (position 1)
- Dark red block at (30, 18)-(33, 21)
- Green square at (38, 12)-(39, 13)
- Gray block at (30, 8)-(33, 11)
- Yellow square at (20, 24)-(21, 25)
- Gray block at (18, 22)-(23, 27)
The arrow keys just advance the HUD, so they're wasted. I need to focus on MOUSE clicks.
Let me think about what configurations are possible:
1. Red toggle: position 1 (vertical) or position 2 (horizontal)
2. Blue toggle: position 1 (blue at bottom-left) or position 2 (blue at top of middle column)
I've tried all 4 combinations:
- (1, 1): initial state - not solved
- (2, 1): red horizontal, blue at bottom-left - not solved
- (1, 2): red vertical, blue at top - not solved
- (2, 2): red horizontal, blue at top - not solved (current state)
So the toggles alone don't solve the level. Maybe I need to click on objects on the left panel, or click on the targets in a specific sequence, or there's another mechanic I haven't discovered.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the arrow keys DO move something, but not the green square. Maybe there's an invisible cursor or selector that I need to position before clicking.
Actually, looking back at the earlier exploration, I noticed that the arrow keys change a single pixel at the bottom of the board (row 63). This is almost certainly a HUD timer bar. The fact that it changes by 1 cell per arrow key press suggests it's a countdown or progress bar.
But the result also says `gameplay_changed: true` for the RIGHT action. This is strange - if it's just a HUD change, shouldn't it be `gameplay_changed: false` or `no_impact: true`?
Wait, let me re-read the result: `"gameplay_changed": true, "no_impact": false`. So the system thinks the RIGHT action changed gameplay. But the only change was at row 63, col 29, which is at the bottom edge of the board.
Hmm, maybe the HUD bar IS part of the gameplay. Let me look at the bottom row more carefully.
Actually, I realize I've been spending too many actions on exploration. Let me try a different approach: maybe I need to click on the objects on the left panel to move them, or click on specific positions within the target shapes.
Let me try clicking on the green square to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Arrow keys only change the HUD bar at row 63 - they're wasted moves
# Let me try clicking on the green square instead
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 12}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
